metadata.title: Minimum Operations to Reduce X to Zero

learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Loops, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine removing books from either end of a shelf until the total weight removed equals a target weight x. Instead of deciding which books to remove, it's easier to determine which consecutive books should remain on the shelf.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine removing books from either end of a shelf until the total weight removed equals a target weight x. Instead of deciding which books to remove, it's easier to determine which consecutive books should remain on the shelf.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this pattern whenever elements can only be removed from both ends. Convert the problem into finding the longest subarray with a target sum.
learning.signals: Whenever elements can only be removed from both ends, think about keeping the middle subarray instead.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given an integer array nums and an integer x. In one operation, you may remove the leftmost or the rightmost element from the array and subtract its value from x. Return the minimum number of operations required to reduce x exactly to 0. If it is impossible, return -1.

examples.explanation: Remove 2 and 3 from the right. Two operations reduce x to 0.
No sequence of removals can reduce x to exactly 0.

edgeCases: Longest valid subarray in the middle

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Recursively try removing elements from the left or right until x becomes zero.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of removing elements from both ends, think about keeping one contiguous middle subarray. If the remaining subarray has sum = totalSum - x, then the removed elements automatically sum to x. Therefore, find the longest subarray with sum = totalSum - x.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Removing elements from both ends is equivalent to keeping one longest middle subarray.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever elements can only be removed from both ends, think about keeping the middle subarray instead.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Because all numbers are positive, the sliding window expands and shrinks monotonically. Each element enters and leaves the window once, giving linear time complexity.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever elements can only be removed from both ends, think about keeping the middle subarray instead.
commonWrongPaths: Not converting the problem into a longest subarray problem.
Using sliding window when negative numbers exist.
Returning the longest length instead of minimum operations.

hints: Find the longest subarray with sum = totalSum - x.
commonMisconceptions: Not converting the problem into a longest subarray problem.
Using sliding window when negative numbers exist.
Returning the longest length instead of minimum operations.

pattern.name: Longest Subarray with Target Sum
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever elements can only be removed from both ends, think about keeping the middle subarray instead.

approaches.title: Try Every Left and Right Removal
approaches.idea: Recursively try removing elements from the left or right until x becomes zero.

code.java: Recursive solution omitted because of exponential complexity.

approaches.title: Prefix Sum + HashMap
approaches.idea: Compute prefix sums and use a HashMap to find the longest subarray with sum = totalSum - x.
algorithm: Store first occurrence of each prefix sum.
approaches.dryRun: Use prefix sums to detect the target sum.

approaches.title: Sliding Window (Positive Numbers)
approaches.idea: Since all numbers are positive, use a sliding window to find the longest subarray whose sum equals totalSum - x. The minimum operations equal the total length minus the longest valid window.
approaches.intuition: Because all numbers are positive, the sliding window expands and shrinks monotonically. Each element enters and leaves the window once, giving linear time complexity.
algorithm: Use a sliding window to find the longest subarray with sum = target.
Return n - longestLength if found; otherwise return -1.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[1,1,4,2,3]
Total=11
Target=6
Window=[1,1,4] Sum=6 Length=3
Longest=3
Answer=5-3=2

visualization.description: Keep the longest middle subarray instead of choosing elements to remove.
steps: Slide the window to find the target sum.

revisionNotes: Sliding window because values are positive.

interviewFollowUps: Why does keeping the longest subarray work?
Why is sliding window valid only for positive numbers?
How would you solve it if negative numbers existed?
Can you return the removed elements?
interviewTips: Reverse the problem into a longest subarray problem.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Removing elements from both ends is equivalent to keeping one longest middle subarray.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Total Sum → Target → Longest Window → n - Length.

commonMistakes: Not converting the problem into a longest subarray problem.
Using sliding window when negative numbers exist.
Returning the longest length instead of minimum operations.
